results.question: What are the capital expenditure (capex) plans and guidance for the current and next fiscal year?
results.expected_answer: Hiranand Savlani (Q3 FY25 call, in response to Rahul Agarwal): 'I think we should be closing around Rs. 450 crore or so for the full year' (FY25) and for the following year, 'Next year should be somewhere around 250 or so' (FY26), explained by Sandeep Engineer as being lower because 'Kanpur is almost completed' and no major new facility additions are planned, with spending mainly on machinery rather than land/buildings. In the Q2 FY25 call, capex for H1 FY25 was stated as INR282 crores with full-year capex expected to be 'the same line only... now balance will be more than INR100 crores' with Kanpur cited as a driver. In the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani separately noted 'In the last two years alone, we have pumped in close to ₹1000 crore into this expansion activity, particularly capex,' with FY26 capex guided at '250 to 300 crore max because Kanpur is still yet to be completed.'
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf):

- Management stated the total CAPEX guidance for the group is around Rs. 750 crores to Rs. 800 crores for the next financial year. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 5 · FY25)

- For the current fiscal year, the expected CAPEX for Craftsman standalone is about Rs. 550 crores. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 6 · FY25)

- For subsidiaries Sunbeam and DR Axion, CAPEX is not fixed and will be taken as it comes. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 6 · FY25)

- There is an additional potential CAPEX of Rs. 40-50 crores for repair and maintenance of very old equipment at Fronberg, which is under evaluation. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 6 · FY25)

- Management also mentioned a possible land sale in Q3 or Q4 of Sunbeam that could add around Rs. 300 crores to cash inflows, which may aid deleveraging and CAPEX funding. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 5 · FY25)

## Summary

The group’s CAPEX guidance for the next fiscal year is Rs. 750-800 crores. For the current fiscal year, Craftsman standalone expects to spend about Rs. 550 crores, with additional CAPEX for subsidiaries and possible repair work at Fronberg (Rs. 40-50 crores) still being evaluated. Management also anticipates cash inflows from a potential land sale at Sunbeam to support funding.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 5 · FY25  
crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 6 · FY25

Yes. Hi, sir. Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, I have a couple of questions. One is on the
balance sheet working capital. The OCF saw compression due to some working capital
stretch on this consolidated business. So, what is causing that sir for the year?

Management answered:
I didn't understand the question.

Analyst (Mitul Shah) asked:
Sir, second question is on your CAPEX guidance of Rs.750 crores to Rs.800 crores at
a group level. If you can split broadly in terms of standalone and just key two, three
subsidiaries ballpark number?

Management answered:
I think Rs.550 crores for Craftsman is what we expect in the current condition. Between
Sunbeam and DR Axion, we will take it as it comes. We have just budgeted now and
there is some repair and maintenance for a very old equipment at Fronberg, which may
be around Rs.40 crores, Rs.50 crores, may be there, we're evaluating that.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, my question is on the guidance. In last quarter you have
guided around Rs.7,000 crores of revenue and EBITDA would be around Rs.1,100
crores and EBIT would be around Rs.700 crores. Do you maintain that guidance in this?

Management answered:
Yes.

results.question: What capacity additions or plant updates did management detail?
results.expected_answer: Specific figures given in the Q3 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani, in response to Keshav Lahoti): 'This year we have already added 47,000 metric tons in 9 month basis' of pipe capacity; Kanpur plant total capacity will be '60,000' metric tons 'in two phases. First will be 30 and then the second will be another 30' with '30 will be coming to FY26 and 30 will be coming to FY27'; Hyderabad plant total planned capacity was '70,000,' of which 'we already completed I think 25,000 metric tonne.' Separately, in the Analyst Meet, Hiranand Savlani stated capacity has grown at a '10.3% CAGR, while sales are happening at a 13.5% CAGR,' with utilization 'in the 55 to 65 range.' In the Q3 call it was also stated overall capacity increased 'by 36,000 metric tons from 334k to 370k metric tons' during the year.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf):

- Management mentioned that in the aluminum segment, the company is currently operating at around 70-75% capacity utilization.
- The order book in the aluminum segment is strong, and the company plans to increase CAPEX to raise capacity utilization to 80-85% by Q2FY25.
- By Q3FY25, further CAPEX will be required for new orders already in the pipeline.
- For the conventional powertrain business, utilization is around 70%, with an upper limit of 85-90% due to seasonality and market factors.
- No specific new plant construction details were given, but the focus is on CAPEX to increase capacity in aluminum and to meet new order demand.

From the same transcript:

- Management also noted localization of alloy wheel and other parts that were earlier imported from China, now being sourced domestically. This is not a new growth requirement but a replacement of imports with local suppliers.

## Summary

Management detailed capacity additions primarily in the aluminum segment, where they plan to increase CAPEX to raise utilization from about 70-75% to 80-85% by Q2FY25, with further CAPEX planned by Q3FY25 to support new orders. Conventional powertrain capacity utilization is around 70%, with some headroom up to 85-90%. Additionally, alloy wheel and other parts imports from China are being localized, replacing imports with Indian suppliers, though this is not a capacity expansion but a localization effort.

results.question: What updates did management provide regarding new acquisitions, overseas offices, or capacity expansion?
results.expected_answer: Management discussed the acquisition of Al Aziz (Sandeep Engineer, Analyst Meet): rationale was to enter the PE/PP pipe and fittings product line, including German-approved gas fittings and water-line fittings; 'Now we are getting four machines—three are coming, and one more will come later—for making PE and PP pipes in our plants.' No specific investment amount or timeline in months/years was disclosed for this acquisition. The company also opened its 'first overseas office in Dubai' (Hiranand Savlani, Analyst Meet) targeting Middle East/Gulf/Africa exports - no investment figure given. On new geography for Paint, Sandeep Engineer/Hiranand Savlani (Q2 FY25 call) discussed phased launches: Astral Paint launched in Gujarat, then Rajasthan/Jaipur, with plans to enter 'Maharashtra and MP in next 5 to 6 months' - qualitative timeline given but no specific investment figure. UK adhesive technology/backward integration deal was also mentioned (Hiranand Savlani, Q3 call): 'we have just signed the agreement. So in the Q1, that will be available to us' - no investment amount disclosed.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report FY25 and earnings call transcripts, the following updates on acquisitions, overseas offices, and capacity expansion were provided:

### Acquisitions and Overseas Offices
- The company acquired the remaining 24% equity shares of DR Axion India Private Limited on 29th June 2024, making DR Axion a wholly owned subsidiary effective 1st July 2024. (Annual-Report-2025.pdf, p.54)
- The company acquired 100% equity stake of Craftsman Germany GmbH, Germany, thereby acquiring its subsidiary Craftsman Fronberg Guss GmbH, Germany on 22nd July 2024. (Annual-Report-2025.pdf, p.54)
- Management mentioned multiple acquisitions including DR Axion, Sunbeam, and Fronberg, which led to one-time expenses in Q4 FY25. (crafts25Q4.pdf, p.8)

### Capacity Expansion and New Business
- The new powertrain business is a strategic growth area, with the first revenues expected in FY27. The development cycle is 18-24 months, so FY26 will see order book buildup but not significant revenue. (crafts25Q4.pdf, p.3)
- Phase I of the Kothavadi plant for the powertrain business is operational from Q1 FY25. (crafts25Q4.pdf, p.3)
- The machine shop for machining large engine blocks is ready, with trials ongoing and orders in place. This capacity is rare globally, placing the company in an exclusive league. (crafts25Q4.pdf, p.3)
- The acquisition of Fronberg Foundry in Europe has enhanced global capability and capacity for large engine blocks. (crafts25Q4.pdf, p.3)
- The storage business is expected to grow at nearly 20% in the next year, indicating capacity or market expansion in that segment. (crafts25Q4.pdf, p.9)

## Summary

Management updated that during FY25, the company completed acquisitions making DR Axion a wholly owned subsidiary and acquiring Craftsman Germany GmbH along with its subsidiary Fronberg Guss GmbH. They also highlighted capacity expansion with the operationalization of the Kothavadi plant and machine shop for large engine blocks, supported by the Fronberg Foundry acquisition, positioning the company uniquely in the global powertrain and foundry market. Additionally, the storage business is expected to grow strongly, reflecting expansion plans.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · p.54 · FY25  
crafts25Q4.pdf · p.3, p.8, p.9 · FY25

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
And on DR Axion side, what would be the peak revenue in FY 26 or FY 27 -- will it grow
by 10% to 12% or it would be better because of this capacity expansion plan for the
Hyundai?

Management answered:
So, whenever we talk about whether Sunbeam, Craftsman, DR Axion, we have to take
one thing into mind. There is always the question of commodity price aluminum. So,
that will also change the top line a little. But I would say that between DR and Craftsman,
Craftsman will be growing more than 20% CAGR for the next two years, that is FY26,
FY27. That is on the organic side of the conventional business of Craftsman is also
growing. Plus the alloy wheel is also adding to the revenue. On a blended average,
overall we will be 20%-plus. We will have a double-digit growth on DR I would say on a
CAGR basis. But I think 8% to 10% is realistic for FY26 and maybe slightly higher than
10% in FY27, yes.

Analyst (Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Great to hear that. And also on the revenue side for next year, how do you see growth
for the storage business?

Management answered:
High teens I would say, almost 20% we can expect the growth on the storage business.

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Yes, this is fine, sir. You can go ahead for the new business.

Management answered:
So, the new business now, our motive or whatever is the strategy behind the powertrain
business was, we were so heavily dependent on commercial vehicle earlier, very
reasonable extent on farm sector and also construction equipment and that was the
reason and little on the passenger vehicle segment. So, all that continues to be the
mainstay, but now our growth is going to come from not only the revival of these
segments, but also from the stationary engine side. We expect the first revenues to
come in, in FY27 not in '26. In '26, it will be there, the order book is filling up, but the
development cycle time is around 18 to 24 months. So, we will not see any three-digit
revenue, the three-digit revenue will only come in FY27 on the new powertrain business.
But as I mentioned, I think it will peak in 2029, 2030 for $100 million revenue for this
business. So, for that, the phase - I of Kothavadi plant is ready and it has become
operational in Q1. So, we will see some revenue generated from this plant. And the
machine shop for machining the large engine blocks are also ready, and already trials
are going on for various customers, orders are in place. So, you may appreciate these
are very few companies in the world are doing this as a Tier 1 supplier. The OEMs are
normally manufacturing them in-house and I would say less than 10 suppliers across
the globe, including China are there for this sort of activity and there was a limited
capacity. So, we have a global capability and global capacity now after the acquisition
of Fronberg Foundry. And in Europe also, on the foundry side also there are less than
half a dozen foundries who are operational for these large engine blocks and two or
three in the North American region and two or three in China. So, we are in exclusive
league in the foundry outside the OEMs themselves. As I mentioned, OEMs are also
running their own foundries, and the machining, and now because of the massive
increase in demand for the data centers, backup power and mainstream power also is
being used by these generators, the order books for these companies are quite full for
the next three, four years. So, we will see significant growth and that is also prompting
outsourcing, but whatever is said and done, the gestation period is very long. We started

results.question: What EBITDA margin contractions or fluctuations did management report, and what were the reasons?
results.expected_answer: Quantified margin impacts disclosed in the Q2 FY25 call (Hiranand Savlani): consolidated EBITDA fell from 17% (last year) to '15.97%, so precisely 16%,' described as 'a 1% drop on the EBITDA'; consolidated EBITDA in absolute terms fell from 'INR233 crores versus this year, INR219 crores, so almost 6% negative EBITDA growth'; UK adhesive EBITDA swung from '9.7% EBITDA' last year to '2% EBITDA' this quarter, and in a later reference from 'INR9.1 crores' to 'a negative EBITDA of minus INR2 crores'; polymer/PVC price fell 'by almost 13.5%' during the quarter; inventory loss was quantified at 'between INR10 crores to INR15 crores.' In the Q3 FY25 call, pipe EBITDA was maintained/grown, with Sandeep Engineer citing 10.9% EBITDA growth for the pipe vertical despite polymer price volatility, and Analyst Meet figures show GP margin improved 'by 1%' despite an 18% polymer price fall for FY25.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript sources, management discussed EBITDA margin fluctuations in multiple segments and the reasons behind them:

1. **Powertrain Segment (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 3 · FY25):**  
   - Management explained that for about 18 months prior to the last quarter, the powertrain segment experienced margin pressure due to a modernization program, repair and maintenance expenses, and low operating leverage because of underutilized capacity and slow customer ramp-up.  
   - Recently, there has been a slight improvement in operating leverage and capacity utilization, stabilizing margins.  
   - The margin contraction was attributed to these one-time modernization and ramp-up issues, and management expects margins to improve or sustain going forward.

2. **Aluminium Segment - Sunbeam Plant (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 4 · FY25):**  
   - EBITDA margin was around 6-7% in the recent quarter.  
   - Management expects Q1 FY26 to be weak, Q2 and Q4 strong, with Q3 weak again due to plant shifting from Gurgaon to Bhiwadi and consolidation activities.  
   - Full year blended EBITDA margin guidance is 8-10%, indicating some fluctuation quarter to quarter due to operational transitions and cost optimization.

3. **Industrial Segment (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 9 · FY25):**  
   - Margins improved significantly in Q4 FY25.  
   - Reasons include new orders in the automated storage division, better pricing, product costing rationalization, and good traction in static racking.  
   - Management expects FY26 margins to be better than Q4 FY25, though some quarter-to-quarter blips may occur.

4. **Standalone Business - "Others" Segment (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 9 · FY25):**  
   - The "Others" segment showed a one-time impact of Rs.148 crores related to CAPEX and inventory rationalization, which are non-operational and non-profit transactions.  
   - This caused a one-time EBITDA impact but is not reflective of ongoing margin trends.

5. **Other Expenses (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 8 · FY25):**  
   - Management did not provide a clear answer on cost control measures or margin improvement related to other expenses, so no explicit commentary on EBITDA margin contraction or improvement from this angle.

## Summary

Management reported EBITDA margin contractions primarily in the powertrain segment due to a prolonged modernization program, repair and maintenance expenses, and low operating leverage from underutilized capacity and slow customer ramp-up. In the aluminium segment, margins fluctuated due to plant relocation and consolidation activities, with a full-year blended margin guidance of 8-10%. The industrial segment saw margin improvement in Q4 FY25 driven by new orders and cost rationalization, with expectations of further improvement in FY26. Additionally, a one-time non-operational impact affected the "Others" segment EBITDA. Overall, margin fluctuations were linked to operational transitions, capacity utilization, and one-time adjustments.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 3, 4, 8, 9 · FY25

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. Good to see the improvement in the standalone margin. Sir, particularly in
the powertrain segment we have seen improvement this quarter. What led to the
improvement there? And also can you update how the Kothavadi plant order book is
shaping up for the heavy engines and for the FY26 and '27, how do you see the revenue
trends there?

Management answered:
Powertrain in the beginning, except the last quarter, I think for the last maybe almost
18 months, we went through quite a modernization program, repair and maintenance,
which was hitting the expenses I would say, that is one thing. The second thing is the
operating leverage was quite less, capacity utilization was quite less across the old
lines. New lines also were not ramping up. The customers were slow. So, now overall
slight improvement in the operating leverage has happened. That is helping us totally.
There has been from the multinational companies who set up plants in India they have
started to produce with the one- or two-years delay, I would say. And there is some
slight revival in the farm sector and a slight revival in the commercial vehicle sector and
whatever is the downside we had experiencing in the many quarters that has more or
less stabilized now, so that nothing more and whatever we are able to utilize the plants
slightly better and optimize our cost. These margins will be sustained or improved
depending on the quarter of the next financial year, but for the full financial year, we can
expect a better result on the powertrain, i.e., accumulative result even when compared
to the Q4 result. So, the margins will creep up just like the period before we did this
massive correction on the powertrain. So, any questions on the fundamental powertrain
business, then I will come to the new powertrain business.

Analyst (Please go ahead.
Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity and congrats for the strong set of numbers, sir. Sir, my first
question is on the aluminum side. So, on Sunbeam, how the quarterly run rate of
revenue and EBITDA margin would be in for 26? So, in this quarter we have done
around Rs.300 crores kind of the numbers and EBITDA is around 6% to 7%. So, how
this revenue and EBITDA would be in at the end of FY26?

Management answered:
So, Q1 will be weak, Q2 will be strong, Q3 will be weak, Q4 will be the most strong on
both on the revenue and EBITDA numbers. So, there are three, four factors. The plant
shifting from Gurgaon to Bhiwadi will be completed by Q2 latest I would say. So, there
will be consolidation happening in Q3. So, in Q4, the operating leverage at Bhiwadi will
set in and the cost optimization also will set in and also manpower rationalization will
be completed in Q4, also peak revenue will be generated in Q4. So, on a blended
average we can say between 8% to 10% EBITDA is what we expect for the full financial
year, but Q1 will be quite muted.

metadata.speaker:
Please go ahead.
Abhishek K Jain

chunks.content:
Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. You mentioned in past the margin for the segment can be very good for the
heavy engines part. I just want to understand, I mean this will be more-accretive than
the current margins or how do you see the range of the margin for this business, sir?

Management answered:
See, the current business, you may be aware that we are not backward integrated, and
we have excellent top notch foundry partners who are leaders in India and also the
OEMs themselves, three of the OEMs in India, major OEM's are running their own
foundries where we are getting the casting from. So, it's a mixture of job work and also
we buy the castings from our foundry partners. So, it is an assorted margin what you're
seeing on the current powertrain business whereas the new powertrain business for the
larger engines, yes, it will start also with the sort of job work activity, but the castings
are not being produced in India, 95% of them, there is only one or two small suppliers
here. So, we are getting the castings from Europe and US to start with for the activity
in 2027, while we ramp up our foundry capacity here with the prove out also, the
castings must be validated. So, when we are supplying this material, it looks like as if
the margins will be lower than the conventional powertrain business, but I can assure
you that the margins are in line with the current powertrain business as in formula wise
how we calculate. When we are buying the castings from elsewhere there cannot be a
margin on the castings, but we make our castings, it will be there, but the margin on the
castings surely it will be slightly lower than the machining because machining is a value
addition, there is no material cost involved. So, it optically look like a higher margin. So,
on the blended average we are on par with whatever we are currently doing.

Analyst (Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Got it, sir. Sir, this quarter, the storage, I mean the industrial segment margins have
seen a lot of improvement in Q4. Can you explain what led to the improvement there?
And going ahead for the next year how do you see the margin for this business, sir?

Management answered:
See, you may be aware of this, but I would like to reiterate one particular point on the
storage business. This is not at all CAPEX-intensive, there is CAPEX, but compared to
the automotive business on both aluminum and powertrain, this is a negligible CAPEX
I would say and working capital also the total capital employed is quite low. So, even
though margins may be low, once we see the traction what we got in Q4, which will
continue into the next financial year, of course, there can be some blips in quarter-to-
quarter but overall we can expect the results for FY26 will be better than the results of
Q4 of FY25. And the reason for that is the automated storage division is getting new
orders because we have made enough inroads into the market, we have enough
projects which have been implemented to showcase to our new incoming customers
and we are getting the right pricing for the product, #1. #2, I think we also have
rationalized the product costing and also on the static racking we are having good
traction there. So, as a blended Company, I think we have matured as a storage solution
Company. This is a very difficult business. Within five years we have come to a level.
This can be really a standalone also possible storage solution business. Not that I'm
implying anything, no, please don't take it that level, but we have grown this business
to be able to stand on its own feet now.

Analyst (Institutional Equities. Please go ahead.
Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Thanks for taking the question again. Sir, in the segmental reporting, there is some line
called others in the standalone business which is around Rs.148 crores. Just can you
explain what is others in the standalone business, sir?

Management answered:
So, this is the one-time subject which has come on to two fronts. One is regarding some
CAPEX which has incurred by Sunbeam, we had to buy the equipment, keep everything
ready because Sunbeam was not ready at that level financially and we transferred the
brand-new machines to them totally. So, for Sunbeam no profit, no loss sale on the
equipment because equipment has to be paid for. Otherwise, we may not be able to do
the shifting of the plant. Second thing was, we rationalized the aluminum alloy inventory,
which was high in Craftsman by transferring at cost to DR Axion. These are the two
one-offs, not a transaction made for profit, it is not a manufacturing activity, so it cannot
be termed as any income.

Institutional Equities. Please go ahead.
Mumuksh Mandlesha

other expenses will come down in the coming quarters?

Management answered:
I didn't get the question.

results.question: What is management's stance on debt reduction, cash utilization, and working capital normalization?
results.expected_answer: No specific debt reduction target was disclosed in any of the three transcripts; Astral is described as net cash positive rather than carrying debt to reduce. Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet): 'Right now, we are sitting on a net cash of ₹464 crore.' On cash utilization/dividend, in the Q2 FY25 call, Pinaki Banerjee asked how the company plans to utilize its cash of 'around Rs. 290 crores'; Hiranand Savlani responded: 'I think by year end we will be able to know what is our next year budget. Based on that we will work out our cash utilization plan and if we don't have any such kind of utilization plan then we will give it to our shareholders' - no specific dividend payout ratio or percentage was committed. On working capital, Hiranand Savlani (Analyst Meet) noted creditor/debtor/inventory day movements tied to a one-off rupee-appreciation-driven early creditor payoff in March, but gave no specific working-capital-day normalization target or timeline in days.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript excerpts:

- On working capital accounting, management clarified that certain amounts are not accounted as working capital but remain as cash (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 10 · FY25).
- Regarding cash utilization and debt, management explained that money infused into the company was used primarily for settling vendor liabilities and some bank liabilities left by previous owners. The shares were bought at Re.1, and the infusion was not a purchase price but capital put into the company to settle obligations (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 8 · FY25).
- There is no explicit mention of ongoing or future debt reduction targets or strategies in the retrieved context.
- Management confirmed maintaining revenue and profitability guidance but did not comment specifically on working capital normalization or further cash deployment plans beyond the settlement described (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 7 · FY25).

## Summary

Management’s stance is that cash infusion was primarily used to settle vendor and some bank liabilities left by previous owners, with no amount accounted as working capital but held as cash. They have not indicated any further debt reduction plans or working capital normalization measures beyond this settlement. Cash utilization has been focused on cleaning up legacy liabilities rather than operational working capital adjustments.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 8 · FY25  
crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 10 · FY25  
crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 7 · FY25

Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
Okay. But it has been accounted in working capital for us, particularly this year?

Management answered:
No, no, that is not accounted. That is still lying as cash.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, my question is on the guidance. In last quarter you have
guided around Rs.7,000 crores of revenue and EBITDA would be around Rs.1,100
crores and EBIT would be around Rs.700 crores. Do you maintain that guidance in this?

Management answered:
Yes.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
We have already taken Rs.160 crores provisioning in Quarter 3 or Quarter 4?

Management answered:
At opening balance itself, when you took over the company. What we need to
understand is, we never paid anything for the company per se, it was infused into the
company, it is not for buying any share. Shares are bought at Re.1. That is it. So, all
the money what we have put into the company has gone into the company for the
settlement of maybe some vendors and things like that. Even most of the liability of the
banks were settled by the erstwhile owners and some of the bank liabilities was left
behind which we funded was in lieu of the land sale of Gurgaon.

results.question: What are the reportable business segments of the group, and what was their segment revenue for the current vs previous year?
results.expected_answer: Astral Limited (Group) has two reportable business segments — 'Plumbing' and 'Paints and Adhesives'. Segment revenue for FY25 (year ended March 31, 2025) vs FY24: Plumbing — ₹41,963 Million (FY24: ₹41,420 Million); Paints and Adhesives — ₹16,361 Million (FY24: ₹14,994 Million); Total segment revenue — ₹58,324 Million (FY24: ₹56,414 Million). Note: this segment disclosure is presented only in the Consolidated Financial Statements; the Standalone Financial Statements (Note 41) state that no segment disclosure is given standalone since it is presented in the consolidated statements (Ind AS 108).
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report 2024-25 (pages 171, 172, 238), the reportable business segments of the group are:

1. Powertrain
2. Aluminium Products
3. Industrial & Engineering
4. Others (not significant, categorized separately)

Segment revenue for the years ended March 31, 2025 (current year) and March 31, 2024 (previous year) are as follows (₹ in Lakhs):

- Powertrain:  
  - FY24-25: Not explicitly stated in the retrieved context for revenue, but segment profit/loss and assets/liabilities are given.  
  - However, from page 237-239, segment revenue is disclosed as part of segment reporting but exact revenue numbers per segment are not explicitly provided in the retrieved context.

The retrieved context does not explicitly provide segment revenue figures broken down by segment for FY24-25 and FY23-24. The context mentions segment profit or loss and segment assets/liabilities but does not explicitly state segment revenue values.

The only explicit segment revenue data mentioned is in the notes about segment revenue being disclosed as per Ind AS 108 and SEBI circular, but the exact segment revenue numbers are not present in the retrieved context.

## Summary

The reportable business segments of the group are Powertrain, Aluminium Products, Industrial & Engineering, and Others. However, the exact segment revenue figures for the current year (FY24-25) and previous year (FY23-24) are not disclosed in the retrieved context.

results.question: What key business risks does the company identify in its Risk Management framework, and what are their mitigation measures?
results.expected_answer: Not disclosed as a single itemized list in that exact section beyond the categories shown in the 'Risk Management' framework: Market Competition; Technological Relevance; Environmental and Social Responsibility; Talent Acquisition and Retention; Supply Chain Continuity; Product Quality and Safety; Intellectual Property Protection; Compliance and Regulatory Adherence. For each, a corresponding mitigation measure is listed (e.g., for 'Market Competition': advance innovation/enhance product offerings, strengthen distribution reach, prioritise operational efficiency through cost management).
results.answer:
## Details

The company identifies several key business risks in its Risk Management framework as detailed in the FY25 Annual Report:

1. **Competition Risk**  
   - The company operates in competitive environments in some segments.  
   - Mitigation: Maintaining multiple sources for products/services and closely monitoring the market with strategies to stay ahead of competition. (Page 6, 9)

2. **Economic Risk**  
   - The economy is vulnerable due to global trade tensions, protectionism, fiscal issues, and a weak financial sector.  
   - Mitigation: Diversification of products, services, markets, and customers to reduce dependence on any single economic factor. (Page 9)

3. **Automobile Industry Cyclicality**  
   - The automobile industry is cyclical and sensitive to interest rates and liquidity.  
   - Mitigation: Increasing the share of Industrial & Engineering segment to reduce exposure to automobile cyclicality. (Page 6)

4. **Product Safety and Quality Risk**  
   - Product defects can lead to recalls, damage to brand equity, customer attrition, and regulatory penalties.  
   - Mitigation: Maintaining product safety and standards to ensure customer trust and competitiveness. (Page 68)

5. **Product Design & Lifecycle Management Opportunity**  
   - Integrating sustainability into product design to reduce environmental footprint and open new markets.  
   - Mitigation: Focus on recyclability, lower emissions, and efficient end-of-life management. (Page 68)

6. **Corporate Governance & Risk Management**  
   - Weak governance or inadequate risk management can lead to compliance violations, financial misstatements, and loss of investor confidence.  
   - Mitigation: Robust governance structures, risk management frameworks, and continuous monitoring. (Page 68)

7. **Occupational Health and Safety Risk**  
   - Risks from accidents, exposure to hazardous materials, ergonomic issues, and regulatory sanctions.  
   - Mitigation: Developing a strong safety culture, conducting regular safety audits, providing PPE and training, and obtaining certifications like ISO 45001. (Page 68)

8. **Climate Change Risk and Opportunity**  
   - Physical risks from extreme weather events affecting manufacturing, infrastructure, supply chains, and employee safety.  
   - Mitigation: Transition to a low-carbon economy, energy audits, innovation in cleaner technologies, and access to green funds. (Page 67)

9. **Supply Chain Risk**  
   - Risks related to supplier performance and ESG compliance.  
   - Mitigation: Supplier Code of Conduct emphasizing ESG criteria, supplier ESG assessments, preference for local and certified vendors, and monitoring supplier ESG KPIs. (Page 70)

10. **Reputational and Regulatory Risk**  
    - Risks of regulatory penalties and reputational loss due to non-compliance or unethical practices.  
    - Mitigation: Strong Code of Ethics, Anti-Corruption Policy, transparency in business operations, and periodic ethics and compliance training. (Page 70)

11. **Financial Risks (Market, Credit, Liquidity)**  
    - Exposure to market risks including foreign currency and interest rate fluctuations.  
    - Mitigation: Use of derivative financial instruments to hedge risks, with oversight and approval by the Board of Directors. (Pages 4, 5)

## Summary

The company identifies key business risks including competition, economic volatility, automobile industry cyclicality, product safety, governance, occupational health and safety, climate change, supply chain, reputational, and financial risks. Mitigation measures include diversification, robust governance and risk frameworks, safety culture and certifications, sustainability integration, supplier ESG management, ethical policies, and financial hedging through derivatives.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 20, 67, 68, 70 · FY25

results.question: What transactions with related parties were disclosed in the financial statements, and what was the compensation of key management personnel?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 37 (Related Party Disclosures) discloses transactions with Subsidiaries, Joint Venture, Enterprises over which KMP have significant influence, KMP, and Relatives of KMP for FY25 vs FY24 (₹ in Million), including: CSR Expenditure – Astral Foundation 126 (108); Interest Income – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 43 (19); Investment in Subsidiaries – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (2,773); Redemption of debentures – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (1,940); Purchase of Goods/Services – Kairamya Journeys LLP 141 (130); Reimbursement of expenses claimed – Astral Coatings Private Limited 60 (49); Remuneration – Sandeep Engineer 152 (139), Hiranand Savlani 59 (42), Others 57 (52); Rent Paid – Jagruti Sandeep Engineer 1 (1), Sandeep Engineer HUF 1 (1); Sale of Goods – Astral Coatings Private Limited 19 (19); Sitting Fees to Independent Directors (0–1 each); Loans Given – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 477 (91); Loans Repaid – Astral Coatings Private Limited – (8); year-end balances: Loans Given outstanding – Seal IT Services Limited, UK 876 (377); Interest accrued on loan – 20 (5); Payables – Sandeep Engineer 47 (45), Others 5 (4); Advance for purchase of non-current investment – Astral Pipes Limited 109 (109). results.answer:
## Details

- According to the Annual Report 2024-25 (pages 8, 14, 15, 20, 21, 232, 233), the Company disclosed that:

  - There were no material related party transactions requiring shareholders’ approval under Section 188 of the Companies Act or Listing Regulations during FY25.  
  - All related party transactions were in the ordinary course of business and on an arm’s length basis.  
  - The Company has a policy on Related Party Transactions available on its website.  
  - Related party transactions and balances are disclosed in the notes to the financial statements pursuant to IND AS-24.

- Specific transactions and balances disclosed (Annual Report pages 20, 21, 232, 233):

  - Purchase of Goods & Services from related parties: ₹4 lakhs (FY24-25)  
  - Sale of Goods & Services to related parties: ₹19.30 lakhs (FY24-25)  
  - Reimbursement of Expenditure to related parties: ₹39 lakhs (FY24-25)  
  - Reimbursement of Expenditure from related parties: ₹36 lakhs (FY24-25)  
  - Interest Income from related parties: ₹34 lakhs (FY24-25)  
  - Rental Income from related parties: ₹6 lakhs (FY24-25)  
  - Management Services Income from related parties: ₹100 lakhs (₹1 crore) (FY24-25)  

- Compensation of Key Management Personnel (KMP) (Annual Report pages 13, 20, 232):

  - Remuneration to KMP (including Chairman & Managing Director, Whole Time Director, CFO, COO, Company Secretary) is disclosed but gratuity contributions are excluded.  
  - The exact remuneration figures for KMP for FY25 are provided in the Annual Return available on the Company’s website (not disclosed verbatim in the retrieved context).  
  - However, the summary table on page 21 shows remuneration to key management personnel as ₹501 lakhs (₹5.01 crore) for FY24-25.

## Summary

The Company disclosed related party transactions during FY25 including purchases (₹4 lakhs), sales (₹19.30 lakhs), reimbursements (₹39 lakhs and ₹36 lakhs), interest income (₹34 lakhs), rental income (₹6 lakhs), and management services income (₹100 lakhs), all on an arm’s length basis and in the ordinary course of business. The total remuneration paid to key management personnel in FY25 was ₹501 lakhs (₹5.01 crore), excluding gratuity contributions.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 8, 13, 14, 15, 20, 21, 232, 233 · FY25

results.question: What operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions did the company detail in the annual report?
results.expected_answer: Directors' Report states that during FY25 the Company increased its installed capacity of the plumbing business by 14.34%, from 3,34,040 MT to 3,81,957 MT, with sales volume rising from 2,19,590 MT to 2,27,090 MT, and incurred capital expenditure of ₹6,177 Million towards plant & machinery, factory building, and other capex. The Letter from the Whole Time Director and CFO (Strategic Review) states nearly ₹1,000 crore was invested in new plants and machinery over the past two years, focused on decentralising manufacturing, and that planned capital expenditure of around ₹300 crore is budgeted for FY26, primarily to complete the Kanpur facility and add further adhesive lines at Dahej. Additionally, the Board approved (April 17, 2025) the acquisition of 100% equity of Al-Aziz Plastics Private Limited (completed May 5, 2025), as part of the strategy to expand product portfolio and enter new high-growth market segments.
results.answer:
## Details

The retrieved context from the Annual Report 2025 does not explicitly mention any specific operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions undertaken by the company during the year. The available sections include:

- Financial performance highlights (Page 18) with turnover, PBT, PAT, and EBITDA figures.
- Notes on depreciation, amortization, and impairment (Pages 150, 217) which indicate capital asset usage but do not detail new capital expenditures or expansions.
- Key audit matters related to Property, Plant and Equipment (Page 108) discuss accounting and valuation but do not describe new capacity or acquisitions.
- Information on subsidiaries and joint ventures (Page 179, 199) provides financial data but no mention of new acquisitions or expansions.
- Strategy and diversification goals (Page 18) mention broad objectives like "Broaden Business Base through Strategic Diversification Initiatives" and "Improve Profitability in Aluminium and Storage Solutions Businesses," but no concrete details on specific projects or capital investments.
- Notes on accounting policies and segment reporting (Pages 111, 191) describe the business segments and accounting treatments but do not disclose new capacity or acquisitions.

No explicit mention of capital expenditure amounts, new plant commissioning, capacity expansions, or strategic acquisitions is found in the provided context.

## Summary

The annual report context provided does not disclose any specific operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions undertaken by the company during the year.

results.question: What Key Audit Matters (KAM) did the independent auditor identify, and what was the auditor's response to them?
results.expected_answer: The Independent Auditor's Report (Standalone) identifies two Key Audit Matters: (1) Impairment assessment of Goodwill (Note 2(x)(iv)) — Goodwill of ₹2,036 Million as at March 31, 2025, tested annually via discounted cash-flow models of Cash Generating Units (CGUs); flagged as a KAM due to judgmental assumptions on projected revenue growth, operating margins, cash flows, and discount rates. Auditor's response: assessed CGU definitions for compliance, evaluated management's future cash flow forecasts, compared forecasts to actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures. (2) Impairment assessment of investments in subsidiaries (Note 2(x)(iii)) — investments in Astral Coatings Private Limited and Seal IT Services Limited, UK amounting to ₹3,707 Million as at March 31, 2025; flagged as KAM due to judgment in determining value-in-use dependent on management estimates of future performance and cash flows. Auditor's response: evaluated future cash flow forecasts used to compute recoverable amount, compared to business plan/actual results, focused on sales growth/discount rate assumptions, recalculated estimates, involved valuation specialists, and assessed disclosures.
results.answer:
## Details

The independent auditor identified the following Key Audit Matters (KAM) for the current period audit of Craftsman Automation Limited and its consolidated financial statements for FY25:

1. **Accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE)**  
   - The capitalisation of PPE includes the fair value of the assets acquired (including rebates) and any directly attributable costs of bringing the asset to its working condition, excluding non-refundable purchase taxes and duties.  
   - Depreciation is charged as per Schedule II to the Companies Act, 2013.  
   - The audit procedures included:  
     - Testing controls and operating effectiveness of internal controls over PPE.  
     - Verifying the capitalisation of self-constructed PPE.  
     - Assessing depreciation rates applied.  
     - Reviewing management’s impairment assessment and valuation judgments.  
     - Verifying additions with underlying documentation to assess compliance with accounting standards.

2. **Accounting for Business Combinations**  
   - The audit focused on:  
     - Assessing terms of acquisition agreements and verifying acquisition dates.  
     - Reviewing calculation of total purchase consideration and evaluating fair value measurements.  
     - Assessing methodologies and assumptions used for identifiable assets acquired and liabilities assumed.  
     - Verifying computation of goodwill or gain on bargain purchase and reasonableness of assumptions.  
     - Reviewing disclosures in consolidated financial statements for compliance with Ind AS 103 (Business Combinations).

The auditor’s response to these KAMs involved detailed substantive procedures and controls testing to ensure the accuracy and compliance of the accounting treatments and disclosures related to these matters.

Additional points from the auditor’s report include:  
- The auditor obtained all necessary information and explanations.  
- Proper books of accounts were maintained.  
- The financial statements were in agreement with the books of account.  
- The auditor complied with ethical requirements and independence standards.  
- The auditor communicated significant audit findings and any deficiencies in internal control to those charged with governance.  
- The auditor confirmed that provisions for material foreseeable losses on long-term contracts, including derivatives, were made as required.  
- No amounts were due for transfer to the Investor Education and Protection Fund.

## Summary

The independent auditor identified two Key Audit Matters for FY25: (1) accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment, focusing on capitalisation, depreciation, and impairment judgments; and (2) accounting for Business Combinations, focusing on acquisition terms, fair value measurement, goodwill computation, and disclosures. The auditor responded with thorough audit procedures including controls testing, substantive verification, and compliance reviews, and communicated these findings to governance while maintaining independence and ethical standards.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 108-111, 175-182 · FY25

results.question: What are the top three largest line items under 'Other Expenses' in the standalone financial statements, and how did they change YoY?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 30 (Other Expenses), the top 3 largest line items for FY25 (year ended March 31, 2025) vs FY24: (1) Advertisement and Sales Promotions expenses — ₹1,921 Million (FY24: ₹2,197 Million), a decrease of ₹276 Million; (2) Freight and Forwarding — ₹1,507 Million (FY24: ₹1,357 Million), an increase of ₹150 Million; (3) Power and Fuel — ₹1,279 Million (FY24: ₹1,268 Million), an increase of ₹11 Million. Total Other Expenses: ₹7,801 Million (FY24: ₹7,661 Million).
results.answer:
## Details

The question asks for the top three largest line items under "Other Expenses" in the standalone financial statements and their year-on-year (YoY) changes.

- The retrieved context includes detailed line items such as Raw Material Cost, Change in Inventory, Power and Fuel, Other Manufacturing Expenses, Employee Cost, Selling and Admin Expenses, Other Expenses, etc.
- However, the line item "Other Expenses" in the profit & loss data is presented as a single aggregated figure each year (e.g., 7.79 Cr in FY2017, 6.23 Cr in FY2018, etc.).
- There is no further breakdown or sub-classification of "Other Expenses" into constituent line items in any of the provided sources (Excel financials, annual report images, PDF text, or earnings call transcripts).
- Therefore, the top three largest line items under "Other Expenses" and their YoY changes are not disclosed in the retrieved context.

## Summary

The retrieved context does not provide a breakdown of "Other Expenses" into individual line items. Hence, it is not possible to identify the top three largest components under "Other Expenses" or analyze their year-on-year changes.

results.question: What new or amended accounting standards were disclosed, and what was their impact?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 2(y) 'New and amended standards' discloses: (1) Ind AS 117 'Insurance Contracts' — notified by MCA on August 12, 2024 under the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The document states the application of Ind AS 117 had no impact on the Company's financial statements, as the Company has not entered into any contracts in the nature of insurance contracts covered under Ind AS 117 (no quantified impact, as impact was nil). (2) Amendment to Ind AS 116 'Leases' — Lease Liability in a Sale and Leaseback, notified via the Companies (Indian Accounting Standards) Second Amendment Rules, 2024, effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after April 1, 2024. The Company does not have such sale-and-leaseback transactions, so the amendment has no impact on its financial statements. Additionally, Note 2(z) states there are no standards notified but not yet effective as on the date of the financial statements.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report FY25, page 123, under the section "C. RECENT ACCOUNTING DEVELOPMENTS," it is explicitly stated:

- During the year ended March 31, 2025, the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) has **not notified any new accounting standards or amendments to the existing standards applicable to the company**.

No impact of new or amended accounting standards is mentioned because none were notified or applicable during the year.

## Summary

No new or amended accounting standards were disclosed as applicable during FY25, and therefore, there was no impact reported from any such changes.

results.question: What provisions (current and non-current) for employee benefits or other liabilities were disclosed, and what schedules were provided?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Financial Statements, Note 16 (Provisions) discloses only two line items with opening/closing balances (no separate created/reversed/utilized movement schedule is provided): Non-current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹82 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹50 Million as at March 31, 2024); Current — Provision for Employee Benefits: ₹18 Million as at March 31, 2025 (₹7 Million as at March 31, 2024). Not disclosed in this annual report: a breakup showing amounts created, reversed, or utilized during the year.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report 2024-25 (Standalone) disclosures:

- **Provisions for employee benefits and other liabilities as at March 31, 2025 (in Lakhs):**  
  - Provision for employee benefits - Gratuity: 630  
  - Other Provisions - Provision for Warranty Cost: 280  
  - Other Provisions - Provision for Rejection: 160  
  - **Total Provisions:** 1,070 Lakhs  

- **Movement in Provisions during FY2024-25:**  
  - Warranty:  
    - Opening balance: 230  
    - Expired during the year: 230  
    - Provided during the year: 280  
    - Closing balance: 280  
  - Rejection:  
    - Opening balance: 127  
    - Expired during the year: 127  
    - Provided during the year: 160  
    - Closing balance: 160  

- **Provisions charged during the year (Note 2.7 Other Expenses):**  
  - Warranty: 45 Lakhs (41 Lakhs in FY23-24)  
  - Rejections: 34 Lakhs (-3 Lakhs in FY23-24)  
  - Doubtful debts: 933 Lakhs (-94 Lakhs in FY23-24)  

- **Schedules/Notes provided:**  
  - Note 1.32: Short Term Provisions (disclosing employee benefits and other provisions)  
  - Note 2.7: Other Expenses (disclosing provisions charged during the year)  
  - Movement details of warranty and rejection provisions are explicitly disclosed.  
  - The provisions are split between current and non-current liabilities in the balance sheet notes (exact split not numerically detailed in the retrieved context).  

## Summary

The company disclosed provisions totaling ₹1,070 Lakhs as at March 31, 2025, comprising ₹630 Lakhs for employee benefits (gratuity), ₹280 Lakhs for warranty costs, and ₹160 Lakhs for rejection costs. Detailed schedules on short-term provisions and movements in warranty and rejection provisions were provided in Notes 1.32 and 2.7 of the standalone financial statements.

results.question: Compute the Debt-to-Equity ratio for FY25 and FY26 and assess the change in financial leverage.
results.expected_answer: FY25 D/E = 0.83x. FY26 D/E = 1.11x. Borrowings rose 54% in one year while equity grew only 14%, indicating accelerating debt-funded capex.
results.answer:
## Details
The Debt-to-Equity ratio is given by the formula:

\[
\text{Debt-to-Equity ratio} = \frac{\text{Borrowings}}{\text{Networth}}
\]

From the provided data in Craftsman Auto.xlsx for FY25 and FY26:

- FY25:
  - Borrowings = 2358.18
  - Networth = 2856.74
  - Debt-to-Equity ratio = 0.83 (already computed)

- FY26:
  - Borrowings = 3623.48
  - Networth = 3264.06
  - Debt-to-Equity ratio = 1.11 (already computed)

## Calculation
To verify the ratios:

For FY25:
\[
\frac{2358.18}{2856.74} = 0.8259 \approx 0.83
\]

For FY26:
\[
\frac{3623.48}{3264.06} = 1.1101 \approx 1.11
\]

Change in Debt-to-Equity ratio from FY25 to FY26:
\[
1.11 - 0.83 = 0.28
\]

## Summary
The Debt-to-Equity ratio increased from 0.83 in FY25 to 1.11 in FY26, indicating a rise in financial leverage by 0.28. This suggests the company has taken on more debt relative to its equity in FY26 compared to FY25.

results.question: Calculate the Operating Profit Margin for FY24 and FY25 and comment on the trend. OPM = Operating Profit / Sales × 100
results.expected_answer: FY24 OPM: 19.74%. FY25 OPM: 14.64%. OPM contracted sharply by ~510 bps despite 27.8% revenue growth — a significant margin deterioration.
results.answer:
## Details

Operating Profit Margin (OPM) is defined as:

\[
\text{OPM} = \frac{\text{Operating Profit}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100
\]

Operating Profit can be approximated as EBITDA minus Depreciation (since EBIT = EBITDA - Depreciation).

From the provided data:

- FY2024:
  - EBITDA = 728.61
  - Depreciation = 277.69
  - Sales = 4451.73

- FY2025:
  - EBITDA = 638.35
  - Depreciation = 347.02
  - Sales = 5690.48

Calculate Operating Profit for each year:

- FY2024 Operating Profit = 728.61 - 277.69 = 450.92
- FY2025 Operating Profit = 638.35 - 347.02 = 291.33

## Calculation

\[
\text{OPM}_{FY24} = \frac{450.92}{4451.73} \times 100 = 10.13
\]

\[
\text{OPM}_{FY25} = \frac{291.33}{5690.48} \times 100 = 5.12
\]

These match the given formula results in the context.

## Summary

The Operating Profit Margin declined from 10.13 in FY24 to 5.12 in FY25, indicating a significant decrease in operating profitability relative to sales over the period.

results.question: In FY25, sales grew 27.8% YoY yet PBT fell 39.4% from FY24. Identify all P&L drivers contributing to this PBT collapse and flag audit implications.
results.expected_answer: Key drivers: OPM contracted from 19.74% to 14.64%; Interest rose from ₹174.54 Cr to ₹216.64 Cr; Other Income collapsed from ₹18.09 Cr to ₹0.30 Cr; Depreciation rose by ₹69.33 Cr. Audit flag: OPM compression on high-revenue year may indicate unrecorded liabilities or accelerated cost recognition.
results.answer:
## Details

### Sales and PBT figures (Excel data):
- FY24 Sales: ₹4451.73 Cr  
- FY25 Sales: ₹5690.48 Cr  
- Sales growth = (5690.48 - 4451.73) / 4451.73 = 27.8% (confirmed)

- FY24 Profit Before Tax (PBT): ₹444.69 Cr  
- FY25 Profit Before Tax (PBT): ₹269.65 Cr  
- PBT decline = (444.69 - 269.65) / 444.69 = 39.4% (confirmed)

### Key P&L line items (FY24 vs FY25) from Excel profit_loss sheet:

| Particulars           | FY24 (₹ Cr) | FY25 (₹ Cr) | Change (₹ Cr) | % Change  |
|----------------------|-------------|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| Sales                | 4451.73     | 5690.48     | +1238.75      | +27.8%    |
| Raw Material Cost    | 2521.53     | 3296.73     | +775.20       | +30.7%    |
| Change in Inventory  | 150.22      | 194.66      | +44.44        | +29.6%    |
| Power and Fuel       | 262.51      | 367.57      | +105.06       | +40.0%    |
| Other Mfr. Exp       | 489.04      | 676.95      | +187.91       | +38.4%    |
| Employee Cost        | 288.74      | 436.67      | +147.93       | +51.2%    |
| Selling and admin    | 155.66      | 255.23      | +99.57        | +64.0%    |
| Other Expenses       | 5.64        | 18.98       | +13.34        | +236.5%   |
| Other Income         | 18.09       | 0.30        | -17.79        | -98.3%    |
| Depreciation         | 277.69      | 347.02      | +69.33        | +25.0%    |
| Interest             | 174.54      | 216.64      | +42.10        | +24.1%    |

### Observations on P&L drivers:

- Raw Material Cost and Change in Inventory combined increased by ₹819.64 Cr (30.7% and 29.6% respectively), roughly in line with sales growth, so not a disproportionate cost increase.
- Power and Fuel increased by 40%, higher than sales growth.
- Other Manufacturing Expenses increased by 38.4%, also higher than sales growth.
- Employee Cost increased sharply by 51.2%.
- Selling and Admin expenses increased by 64%, the highest among major expense heads.
- Other Expenses increased dramatically by 236.5%, though the absolute amount is small (₹13.34 Cr increase).
- Other Income dropped sharply by 98.3%, reducing offset to expenses.
- Depreciation and Interest expenses increased by 25% and 24.1% respectively, adding to cost pressure.

### EBITDA and Net Profit:

- EBITDA decreased from ₹728.61 Cr (FY24) to ₹638.35 Cr (FY25), a decline of 12.4%, indicating margin compression before depreciation and interest.
- Net Profit fell from ₹304.47 Cr to ₹194.57 Cr, a 36% decline, consistent with PBT fall.

### Audit implications from Annual Report narrative and audit notes:

- The large increase in Other Expenses (noted in Annual Report page 186) includes significant increases in Stores, Spares & Tools consumed (+₹41 Cr), Power & Fuel (+₹38 Cr), and Payment to Contractors (+₹26 Cr), indicating cost pressures and possibly operational inefficiencies or inflationary impact.
- The audit report (Annual Report page 109 and 120) highlights Key Audit Matters related to valuation of Property, Plant & Equipment (PPE), depreciation rates, and internal controls over capital expenditure and asset capitalization.
- The sharp increase in depreciation (+25%) and capital work in progress (from ₹178.61 Cr to ₹345.26 Cr) suggests significant CAPEX, which requires careful audit scrutiny for capitalization policies and impairment testing.
- Interest expense increased by 24%, consistent with higher borrowings (from ₹1754.82 Cr to ₹2358.18 Cr), raising concerns about debt servicing and going concern risks.
- The decline in Other Income (from ₹18.09 Cr to ₹0.30 Cr) may indicate reduced non-operating income or asset sales, which should be verified for completeness and accuracy.
- The significant increase in Selling and Admin expenses (+64%) and Employee Cost (+51%) may require audit focus on expense recognition and payroll controls.
- The mismatch between strong sales growth and declining PBT suggests margin pressure and cost overruns, which auditors should investigate for potential misstatements or management override risks.

## Summary

In FY25, despite a 27.8% increase in sales, PBT fell 39.4% due to disproportionate increases in operating expenses: power and fuel (+40%), other manufacturing expenses (+38.4%), employee cost (+51.2%), selling and admin expenses (+64%), and other expenses (+236.5%). Additionally, depreciation (+25%) and interest (+24%) rose significantly, compressing profits. Other income declined sharply, removing a cushion. Audit implications include heightened scrutiny on PPE valuation and depreciation, capital expenditure controls, expense recognition, and debt servicing risks given increased borrowings and interest costs.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
In aluminum business, you said that the growth would be 20% CAGR where the DR
Axion growth would be 10% to 12% and there will be incremental revenue from
Sunbeam. Can you please comment on how the growth will come from the standalone
element business?

Management answered:
So, the Q4 aluminum revenue is the right picture because as you understand that
Sunbeam was having only six months of consolidation. So, the Q4 revenue has been
around Rs.1,000 crores overall. So, it will be more than Rs.4,000 crores for the year,
even depending on the seasonality, it is more closer to Rs.4,500 I would say. In the
previous year on the consolidated number, our revenue was Rs.2,150 crores is strictly
not comparable totally. So, in FY 25, we look at the number of Rs.3,000-odd crores and
we are already at a run rate of Rs.1,000 crores per quarter. So, overall our growth will
be on a consolidated basis more than 30%, but it's not an apple-to-apple comparison,
the realistic growth will be on a 20% level.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
And on DR Axion side, what would be the peak revenue in FY 26 or FY 27 -- will it grow
by 10% to 12% or it would be better because of this capacity expansion plan for the
Hyundai?

Management answered:
So, whenever we talk about whether Sunbeam, Craftsman, DR Axion, we have to take
one thing into mind. There is always the question of commodity price aluminum. So,
that will also change the top line a little. But I would say that between DR and Craftsman,
Craftsman will be growing more than 20% CAGR for the next two years, that is FY26,
FY27. That is on the organic side of the conventional business of Craftsman is also
growing. Plus the alloy wheel is also adding to the revenue. On a blended average,
overall we will be 20%-plus. We will have a double-digit growth on DR I would say on a
CAGR basis. But I think 8% to 10% is realistic for FY26 and maybe slightly higher than
10% in FY27, yes.

Analyst (Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Great to hear that. And also on the revenue side for next year, how do you see growth
for the storage business?

Management answered:
High teens I would say, almost 20% we can expect the growth on the storage business.

Analyst (Mitul Shah) asked:
Sir, thank you for giving the opportunity, Sir, my first question is if you can help with the
utilization of various business segments currently for Q4?

Management answered:
I think, yes, on the conventional powertrain business, we have touched around 70%-
odd. So, I think the upper limit will be 85% to 90% because of the seasonality as well
as there will be some customers losing market share or they will have some inventory
correction, which will happen. So, there is the headroom. So, I think I will not say 70,
slightly below 70, very difficult to exactly put a number to it. Even in the aluminum side,
we are at operating at around 70%, 75%, but the order book is quite strong. So, we
need to increase CAPEX. We will quickly touch 80%, 85% by Q2 itself. So, by Q3, we
need CAPEX for the new orders which is already there in the pipeline.

results.question: The company paid zero dividend in FY20 and FY21 despite reporting net profits. Evaluate management's capital allocation consistency across the decade.
results.expected_answer: FY20 dividend skip is explainable (COVID, revenue fell 18%). FY21 skip is more concerning — profits recovered to ₹97 Cr yet no dividend, coinciding with IPO. Post-IPO dividends resumed and scaled. Payout ratios remain low (5-10%), consistent with growth-capex-heavy phase.
results.answer:
## Details

- **Dividend Payment in FY20 and FY21:**
  - From the Excel financials for FY2020 and FY2021 (Sources [1] and [2]), the company reported net profits of ₹40.01 Cr in FY20 and ₹97.36 Cr in FY21.
  - There is no mention of dividend payments in these years in the retrieved context.
  - The Annual Report for FY25 (Sources [7], [9], [15]) explicitly states a dividend declaration for FY25 (final dividend of ₹5 per share, total outlay ₹11.93 Cr), but no dividend is mentioned for FY20 or FY21.
  - The absence of dividend disclosure for FY20 and FY21 combined with explicit dividend declaration in FY25 suggests zero or no dividend was paid in FY20 and FY21 despite profits.

- **Capital Allocation Consistency Across the Decade:**
  - The company reported consistent net profits in FY20 (₹40.01 Cr) and FY21 (₹97.36 Cr).
  - Cash flow from operating activities increased from ₹306.28 Cr in FY20 to ₹362.38 Cr in FY21 (Sources [5], [6]).
  - Borrowings decreased significantly from ₹1058.43 Cr in FY20 to ₹805.98 Cr in FY21 (Sources [3], [4]), indicating possible debt repayment.
  - Net worth increased from ₹721.59 Cr in FY20 to ₹969.43 Cr in FY21, showing retained earnings accumulation.
  - No dividend payout in these years implies management prioritized internal capital retention, possibly for debt reduction or reinvestment.
  - In FY25, the company declared a substantial dividend (₹11.93 Cr), indicating a shift or resumption in capital return to shareholders after a period of retention.

- **Additional Context:**
  - The FY25 Annual Report (Sources [7], [9], [15]) mentions a dividend distribution policy and a dividend payout consistent with that policy.
  - The company raised ₹1200 Cr through QIP in FY25 (Source [18]), which may have influenced capital allocation decisions in prior years to conserve cash.

## Summary

The company did not pay any dividend in FY20 and FY21 despite reporting net profits, reflecting a capital allocation strategy focused on internal retention, debt reduction, and reinvestment during that period. This approach shows consistency in prioritizing balance sheet strengthening over shareholder payouts in the earlier part of the decade, with a resumption of dividends only in FY25.

results.question: Inventory grew from ₹836 Cr in FY23 to ₹1,745 Cr in FY26 — a 109% increase while sales grew 153%. Analyse inventory efficiency and flag any buildup risk.
results.expected_answer: Inventory days improved from 96 days (FY23) to 79 days (FY26) despite absolute inventory doubling. No buildup risk based on disclosed figures. However auditors should verify physical stock counts and provisioning policy.
results.answer:
## Details

- Inventory in FY23 = ₹836 Cr  
- Inventory in FY26 = ₹1,745 Cr  
- Inventory growth = ((1,745 - 836) / 836) * 100 = 109% (as stated)

- Sales growth over the same period = 153% (given)

Inventory grew by 109%, while sales grew by 153%. Since sales growth outpaced inventory growth, this suggests that inventory is not building up disproportionately relative to sales.

Inventory efficiency is often assessed by comparing inventory growth to sales growth. Here, inventory has grown at a slower rate than sales, indicating improved or stable inventory turnover and no immediate risk of inventory buildup.

## Summary

Inventory increased by 109% from ₹836 Cr in FY23 to ₹1,745 Cr in FY26, while sales grew faster at 153%. This indicates no inventory buildup risk, as inventory growth is well controlled relative to sales growth, suggesting maintained or improved inventory efficiency.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · calculation · 2026

results.question: The company shows a negative effective tax rate in FY17 where Net Profit exceeded PBT. Explain the mechanics and flag forensic concerns.
results.expected_answer: FY17: PBT = ₹36.67 Cr; Tax = −₹43.07 Cr; Net Profit = ₹79.74 Cr. Effective tax rate = −117.5%. A tax credit of ₹43 Cr when PBT is only ₹37 Cr inflated Net Profit by 117% — auditors must verify DTA recognition criteria.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Excel financial data for FY2017 (Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2017):

- Profit before tax (PBT): ₹36.67 Cr
- Tax expense: -₹43.07 Cr (negative tax)
- Net profit: ₹79.74 Cr

Here, the net profit (₹79.74 Cr) is higher than the profit before tax (₹36.67 Cr), which is unusual and indicates a negative effective tax rate.

**Mechanics:**

- A negative tax expense means the company recognized a tax benefit rather than a tax charge.
- This can occur due to:
  - Deferred tax asset recognition (e.g., carryforward losses or timing differences).
  - Tax credits or incentives.
  - Reversal of prior year tax provisions.
  - Other non-cash tax adjustments.

However, the exact reason for the negative tax is not disclosed in the retrieved context.

**Forensic Concerns:**

- The negative tax expense leading to net profit exceeding PBT is a red flag that warrants scrutiny.
- Potential concerns include:
  - Aggressive or aggressive tax accounting practices.
  - Recognition of deferred tax assets without sufficient evidence of future taxable profits.
  - Possible misstatement or manipulation of tax provisions.
  - Risk of tax disputes or contingencies not adequately disclosed.
- Without detailed notes or management commentary on tax, this anomaly should be investigated further by auditors or forensic analysts.

## Summary

In FY2017, Craftsman Automation Ltd reported a negative tax expense of ₹43.07 Cr, causing net profit (₹79.74 Cr) to exceed profit before tax (₹36.67 Cr), resulting in a negative effective tax rate. This unusual situation suggests recognition of tax benefits or deferred tax assets but raises forensic concerns about the appropriateness and reliability of tax accounting, warranting further detailed investigation.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2017  
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · balance_sheet · FY2017

results.question: In FY26, Other Expenses in the P&L is reported at ₹1,842.44 Cr — more than 5x the prior year figure of ₹18.98 Cr in FY25. Flag this anomaly and its implications.
results.expected_answer: FY26 Other Expenses: ₹1,842.44 Cr — a 9,603% increase. This is likely a data reclassification anomaly. Cost structure analysis cannot be performed at sub-line level for FY26.
results.answer:
## Details

- From Craftsman Auto.xlsx profit_loss sheet:
  - Other Expenses in FY25 = ₹18.98 Cr
  - Other Expenses in FY26 = ₹1,842.44 Cr

- The increase from FY25 to FY26 is:
  - ₹1,842.44 Cr / ₹18.98 Cr ≈ 97 times increase, which is significantly more than 5x.

- From Annual-Report-2025.pdf notes and other expenses breakdown (FY25):
  - Other Expenses in FY25 are detailed and include manufacturing expenses, power & fuel, administrative expenses, etc., totaling around ₹131.94 Cr (from profit & loss FY25 sheet).
  - However, the standalone P&L in Excel shows Other Expenses as ₹18.98 Cr for FY25, which is much lower than the detailed notes and other expenses line items in the annual report.

- This suggests a possible inconsistency or difference in classification or reporting between FY25 and FY26 in the Excel data or a data entry error.

- The magnitude of increase (from ₹18.98 Cr to ₹1,842.44 Cr) is unusual and warrants investigation.

## Implications

- Such a drastic increase in Other Expenses could indicate:
  - A reclassification of expenses that were earlier reported under different heads now grouped under Other Expenses.
  - One-time or exceptional expenses booked in FY26 but not separately disclosed.
  - Possible data entry or reporting error in the Excel data for FY25 or FY26.
  - If accurate, this would severely impact profitability and operational cost structure, requiring management explanation.

- Without corroborating narrative or management commentary from earnings call transcripts or detailed notes for FY26, the reason for this anomaly cannot be ascertained from the retrieved context.

- This anomaly should be flagged for further audit and management clarification to ensure financial statement reliability and transparency.

## Summary

The reported Other Expenses in FY26 at ₹1,842.44 Cr represent an approximately 97-fold increase over the FY25 figure of ₹18.98 Cr, which is an extreme anomaly. This discrepancy likely indicates a classification change, exceptional item, or data error and requires urgent investigation and management explanation due to its significant impact on financial analysis and profitability assessment.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY25, FY26  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Notes to Standalone Financials · FY25  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Notes to Consolidated Financials · FY25

results.expected_answer: Craftsman acquired a controlling stake (76%) in DR Axion India Private Limited in February 2023. The consideration paid was ₹375 Crores for 76% of total paid-up equity share capital.

From the Annual Report FY23 narrative and highlights sections, the key inorganic highlight disclosed by the company was an acquisition or inorganic growth event. results.question: What were the two Key Audit Matters identified by the statutory auditors of the company for FY24, and what percentage of total assets does PPE represent?
results.expected_answer: Two KAMs: (1) Accounting for derivative contracts — derivative assets ₹943 Lakhs, liabilities ₹259 Lakhs. (2) Accounting for PPE. PPE including CWIP represents 48% of total assets.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report 2023-24 (pages 121 and 187), the two Key Audit Matters (KAMs) identified by the statutory auditors for FY24 are:

1. **Accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE)**  
   - This includes evaluation of initial recognition costs, valuation of PPE, review of useful lives, and periodic physical verification of PPE by management.  
   - The audit procedures involved testing controls over measurement, depreciation rates, and impairment assessments.  
   - PPE is significant due to its material value and the significant judgments and estimates involved in its accounting.

2. **Accounting and Valuation of Derivative Instruments**  
   - The audit focused on valuation of derivatives, compliance with Ind AS 109, and the recognition and derecognition of derivative contracts.  
   - Derivative assets were material to the financial statements, requiring significant judgment and estimates.

Regarding the percentage of total assets represented by PPE:

- The standalone financials note that PPE represents **48%** of the Company’s total assets (Annual Report page 121).  
- The consolidated financials note PPE represents **49%** of the Group’s total assets (Annual Report page 187).

## Summary

The two Key Audit Matters identified by the statutory auditors for FY24 were (1) accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment, including valuation, depreciation, and impairment, and (2) accounting and valuation of derivative instruments under Ind AS 109. Property, Plant and Equipment represent approximately 48% of the Company’s total assets.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2023-24.pdf · page 121 · FY24  
Annual-Report-2023-24.pdf · page 187 · FY24

results.question: In the company's FY22 MD&A, which specific financial ratio showed the highest year-on-year percentage change, and what two factors does management explicitly attribute to this change?
results.expected_answer: The Interest Coverage Ratio showed the highest change at +53% (from 4.17x in FY21 to 6.40x in FY22). Management attributes this to reduction in interest cost from ₹107 Cr to ₹84 Cr and increase in EBITDA from ₹447 Cr to ₹539 Cr.
results.answer:
## Details

From the FY22 Annual Report (Page 93 and Page 121), the ratio analysis comparing FY22 to FY21 shows the following key percentage changes:

- Current Ratio: 7%
- Debt-Equity Ratio: -13%
- Debt Service Coverage Ratio: 28%
- Return on Equity Ratio: 33-34%
- Inventory Turnover Ratio: 0% to -1%
- Trade Receivables Turnover Ratio: 15-16%
- Trade Payables Turnover Ratio: 30%
- Net Capital Turnover Ratio: -1% to -6%
- Net Profit Ratio: 16-17%
- Return on Capital Employed: 20-21%
- Interest Coverage Ratio: +53% (from 4.17x to 6.40x)

Among these, the **Interest Coverage Ratio** shows the highest year-on-year percentage change of **+53%**.

Management explicitly attributes this increase in Interest Coverage Ratio to two factors (Page 93):

1. Reduction in interest cost from ₹107 Crores in FY21 to ₹84 Crores in FY22.
2. Increase in EBITDA from ₹447 Crores in FY21 to ₹539 Crores in FY22.

## Summary

The Interest Coverage Ratio showed the highest year-on-year percentage increase of 53% in FY22. Management attributes this improvement to a reduction in interest cost from ₹107 Crores to ₹84 Crores and an increase in EBITDA from ₹447 Crores to ₹539 Crores.

## Sources

7.-Annual-Report-2021-22.pdf · Page 93 · FY22  
7.-Annual-Report-2021-22.pdf · Page 121 · FY22

results.question: According to the FY25 MD&A, what was management's stated explanation for the significant deterioration in Operating Profit Margin, Net Profit Margin, and Return on Net Worth, and which ratio deteriorated most severely?
results.expected_answer: Management attributed deterioration to one-time expenses for acquisitions and initial losses on greenfield projects. Return on Net Worth deteriorated most severely at 77% — from 13% in FY24 to 3% in FY25.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report FY25 MD&A section (Source: Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 19 · FY25):

- The Operating Profit Margin, Net Profit Margin, and Return on Net Worth all showed significant deterioration.
- The stated reason for the deterioration in Operating Profit Margin was due to "One-time Expenses."
- The deterioration in Net Profit Margin and Return on Net Worth was attributed to a "Decrease in profit after tax" and an "Increase in Equity on account of QIP" (Qualified Institutional Placement).
- The Interest Coverage Ratio increased due to an increase in interest cost.
- The exact percentage changes for these ratios are given on page 239 (Source: Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 239 · FY25):
  - Operating Profit Margin decreased from 22% to 9%, a decline of 13 percentage points (59% decline).
  - Net Profit Margin decreased from 7.6% to 3.5%, a decline of 4.1 percentage points (53% decline).
  - Return on Net Worth decreased from 22% to 12%, a decline of 10 percentage points (43% decline).

Among these, the Operating Profit Margin deteriorated most severely in percentage terms (59% decline).

## Summary

Management explained the significant deterioration in Operating Profit Margin was due to one-time expenses, while the declines in Net Profit Margin and Return on Net Worth were due to decreased profit after tax and increased equity from QIP. The Operating Profit Margin deteriorated most severely, declining by 59%.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 19 · FY25  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 239 · FY25
